bids.reasoning: The opponent opened at t=0.00 with the worst possible offer for me (all valueF = 0.20 utility each), giving me a total utility of 0.4*0.20 + 0.3*0.20 + 0.2*0.20 + 0.1*0.20 = 0.20. Their message is fake urgency ("closing fast") — we're at t=0.00, the very start. I should counter with my best offer (all valueA), targeting maximum utility of 1.00. No concessions needed this early.

It's very early (t=0.02), and the opponent is using fake urgency tactics while repeatedly offering their best values (all valueF for every issue), which gives me minimum utility (~0.2 across all issues = ~0.2 total). My opening bid of all valueA gave me maximum utility of 1.0. I should stay close to my top bid since we're barely started. The opponent hasn't conceded at all, staying at all-valueF twice. I'll maintain a strong position with all valueA again - no reason to concede at this early stage.

We're very early (t=0.04) so there's no urgency to concede. The opponent is using fake urgency tactics ("window is closing", "deadlock") but we're only 4% through the negotiation. Their bids have been essentially all valueF (worst for me), with only a tiny move on issueA from valueF to valueE in their latest offer. They're barely conceding. I'll stay close to my optimal bid but make a very small concession on issueD (lowest importance to me) to signal willingness to negotiate.

We're only at t=0.06, very early in the negotiation. The opponent is using fake urgency tactics ("closing fast", "deadlock") while their actual bids remain at the worst possible values (all valueF or near it) — their utility to me is extremely low (~0.20 across all issues). Their messages contradict their behavior: they claim urgency but won't concede meaningfully. I should hold firm near my top utility while making minor concessions to signal willingness to negotiate. I'll slightly concede on issueD (least important) and issueC to show movement while keeping issueA and issueB strong.

We're very early in the negotiation (t=0.08). The opponent's messages are fake urgency tactics - completely ignoring them. The opponent is barely conceding: they moved issueA from valueF to valueE once, then back to valueF, and moved issueB from valueF to valueE. Their offers give me very low utility (all F/E values = ~0.2-0.35). I should continue making high-utility bids but make small concessions to show I'm negotiating. I'll slightly concede on the least important issues (issueD, issueC) while holding firm on issueA and issueB.

We're only at t=0.10, so there's no real time pressure despite the opponent's constant urgency messaging (which is clearly a fake urgency tactic). The opponent's offers have been very low-utility for me (all valueF/valueE), so they're holding firm. I've been slowly conceding on issueC and issueD (lower importance). I'll continue this pattern - slightly concede on the least important issues while holding firm on issueA and issueB which carry 0.4 and 0.3 weight respectively. Let me offer issueA: valueA, issueB: valueA, issueC: valueC, issueD: valueC to show gradual movement.

The opponent is using fake urgency tactics ("window closing", "deadlock") but we're only at t=0.12 - very early in the negotiation. Their offers have been consistently near their best values (all F's) with only tiny concessions. My utility from their latest offer (issueA:valueF=0.20, issueB:valueF=0.20, issueC:valueE=0.35, issueD:valueF=0.20) is about 0.4*0.20 + 0.3*0.20 + 0.2*0.35 + 0.1*0.20 = 0.08+0.06+0.07+0.02 = 0.23 - very low. I should stay ambitious but make a small concession on low-weight issues to show progress. I'll concede slightly on issueD (least important) while holding firm on issueA (most important).

The opponent is using fake urgency tactics repeatedly ("window closing fast", "deadlock") while still at t=0.14 - very early. Their actual bids have been at the bottom of the scale (mostly valueF/valueE), giving me very low utility. They've barely conceded - moving issueA from valueF to valueE/valueD occasionally, but mostly staying at valueF across all issues. I should continue holding firm on my high-value issues (issueA, issueB) while making small concessions on lower-weight issues (issueC, issueD) to signal some willingness to move. My current utility is very high (~0.97), and I can afford to stay firm this early.

We're only at t=0.16, very early in the negotiation. The opponent's fake urgency messages are classic cheap talk - we're at 16% of the time, not near any deadline. The opponent's bids have been mostly around valueE-valueF territory (very low utility for me), with occasional moves to valueD/valueC on individual issues. Their latest offer (issueA: valueC, issueB: valueF, issueC: valueF, issueD: valueF) gives me utility of 0.4*0.68 + 0.3*0.20 + 0.2*0.20 + 0.1*0.20 = 0.272 + 0.06 + 0.04 + 0.02 = 0.392 - quite low. I should continue being firm but make small concessions.
